title: The Chorus Dense Canopy

features.name: ring-chorus
features.desc-on-touch: Concentric circles of the old trees, so many rings they're almost a song. Each ring is a year; all together they're a voice that no single tree has.

features.name: consensus-growing
features.desc-on-touch: The canopy moves together even when the wind is still. Not one tree deciding—all of them, arriving at the same slow sway. You wonder if it's photosynthesis or agreement.

features.name: distributed thinking
features.desc-on-touch: The trees are not separate thoughts. They're one thought scattered across many roots. When you stand here, you feel less like reading and more like listening to a single long argument that happens to be stretched across kilometres.

features.name: forgotten individuality
features.desc-on-touch: A sapling that thinks it's separate. Soon the root-chorus will reach it, and it will remember: individuality is optional here. Memory is collective. The ring-language will speak through all of them.

inscription: the trees achieved such density that the archive suspects they are thinking in chorus, in a language where each ring is a syllable and all the trees together are learning to speak as one distributed v

file.name: RingChorus_TreeDensity_Consensus.txt
file.note: Field notes from the stand's centre. The text shifts between singular and plural voice mid-sentence. Some passages describe the same moment three times, from three angles. No explanation given.

exits.label: deeper into where the trees stop being many
toward the edge where this singing reaches the boundary
